resume:
MARCUS DELANEY
Senior Manufacturing Operations Manager | Lean & Continuous Improvement
Greenville, SC | marcus.delaney.mfg@example.com

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Senior operations leader with seventeen years in discrete and process manufacturing, spanning automotive tier-one supply, industrial pumps, and most recently medical-device contract manufacturing. I have run multi-line plants, stood up new value streams, and led plant-wide lean transformations grounded in the Toyota Production System. My background is hands-on: I came up through the maintenance and quality functions before moving into production management, so I am comfortable on the floor solving a takt-time imbalance and equally comfortable in a steering-committee room defending a capital request. I have managed direct organizations of up to 220 people across three shifts, owned plant P&L responsibility approaching forty million dollars, and consistently delivered safety, quality, delivery, and cost improvements through structured problem solving rather than firefighting. I believe operational excellence is a daily management discipline, not a program, and I have spent my career building the management systems — tiered accountability, layered process audits, standard work — that make improvement stick after the consultants leave.

EXPERIENCE

Plant Operations Manager — Cardinal MedTech Manufacturing, Greenville, SC (2019 to present)
Lead site operations for an FDA-registered, ISO 13485 medical-device contract manufacturer producing single-use surgical instruments and fluid-management assemblies. Accountable for production, maintenance, materials, and the on-floor quality technicians across two buildings and three shifts; roughly 180 direct and indirect reports. Own the site operations budget and the annual cost-out commitment to corporate.
- Led the plant through a complex product transfer when a sister site closed, absorbing four product families and the associated tooling, validation packages, and tribal knowledge without a customer-facing line-down event. This is the work I most often get asked about in detail.
- Stood up a daily tiered management system (Tier 1 cell huddles through Tier 3 site review) that cut the average time-to-escalate a line stoppage from a shift-and-a-half to under two hours.
- Drove first-pass yield on the highest-volume catheter assembly line from the low eighties into the mid nineties over about eighteen months through a combination of fixture redesign, operator certification, and tighter incoming-inspection criteria on a problem resin.
- Reduced overtime spend materially during a demand surge by rebalancing line staffing against takt and cross-training a flex pool, while holding to the validated process.
- Chaired the site CAPA review and partnered closely with Quality and Regulatory; I am fluent in working inside a controlled, audited environment and have hosted FDA and notified-body audits.

Production Manager — Hollis Industrial Pumps, Charlotte, NC (2014 to 2019)
Ran the machining and assembly value streams for centrifugal and positive-displacement pumps in a make-to-order environment with heavy product mix. Managed about 95 hourly associates and six salaried supervisors across two shifts.
- Led a value-stream-mapping kaizen series that compressed order-to-ship lead time on the flagship pump line from roughly nine weeks to five by attacking queue time between machining and assembly, not just touch time.
- Implemented a pull system with sized supermarkets between the machine shop and final assembly, which cut work-in-process inventory substantially and freed floor space later used for a new test cell.
- Owned the SQDC tiered boards and coached supervisors on root-cause problem solving (5-why, fishbone, basic SPC interpretation) rather than blame.
- Survived a rough product-launch ramp where I learned, painfully, how not to sequence a capacity expansion against an unstable supplier base.

Continuous Improvement / Lean Manager — Apex Driveline Systems, Spartanburg, SC (2010 to 2014)
Tier-one automotive supplier of driveline components to two major OEMs. Plant-level lean leader reporting to the plant manager, with a dotted line to the corporate operating-system office.
- Facilitated more than forty kaizen events over four years covering setup reduction (SMED), line balancing, 5S, and standardized work; trained the plant's first cohort of internal lean facilitators.
- Led an SMED effort on a high-changeover stamping press that took changeover from over ninety minutes to under thirty, unlocking smaller batch sizes and better mix flexibility.
- Supported the launch of the corporate production system, including the layered process audit standard that later spread to two other plants.

Maintenance & Quality roles — Apex Driveline Systems (2007 to 2010)
Started as a reliability technician, moved into a quality-engineering role owning gauge R&R, PPAP submissions, and supplier corrective actions before transitioning into continuous improvement. This early grounding in measurement-system integrity and supplier quality still shapes how I read shop-floor data today.

KEY STRENGTHS
Toyota Production System and lean daily management; tiered accountability and standard work; value-stream mapping and pull-system design; SMED and changeover reduction; first-pass-yield and scrap reduction; ISO 13485 / FDA-regulated operations; CAPA and root-cause facilitation; capital justification and project sequencing; cross-shift leadership and operator development.

EDUCATION & CERTIFICATIONS
B.S., Industrial Engineering — Clemson University (2007)
Lean Six Sigma Black Belt — ASQ
Certified in TWI Job Instruction and Job Relations

NOTE ON STYLE
I lead from the floor, manage by the numbers, and develop people deliberately. I am most proud not of any single metric but of supervisors I have coached who went on to run their own plants.
jd: Senior Operations Manager — Medical Device Manufacturing (ISO 13485). A precision contract manufacturer is hiring a Senior Operations Manager to run a 200-person, three-shift site producing single-use surgical and fluid-management devices. You will own SQDC performance for the site, lead the daily management system, and be accountable for first-pass yield, on-time delivery, and the annual cost-out target in a fully validated, FDA-audited environment. A near-term priority is leading the transfer-in of two product families from a consolidating sister plant with zero customer disruption. We are looking for a leader who can describe, in granular detail, how they personally drove a hard operational outcome — the specific sequence of actions, who owned what, and the measured result — not just the philosophy behind it.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current role at Cardinal MedTech — scope, headcount, and what you're accountable for.
You mention a product transfer when a sister site closed. Set the scene: how many SKUs, what timeline, what was at stake?
What made this transfer hard specifically — was it the validation work, the tooling, or the knowledge transfer?
Walk me through how you sequenced it. What moved first?
Who owned the validation packages — you, Quality, or the sending site?
How did you protect the customer from a line-down while standing up the new lines?
Tell me about a point where the transfer was at risk. What specifically went wrong?
What did you personally do in that moment, that week?
Let's talk yield. You drove first-pass yield up on the catheter line — what was the starting point?
What was the dominant defect mode you were attacking?
Walk me through the fixture redesign — what changed mechanically?
How did you isolate the resin as a contributor versus the fixture?
Over what period did yield move, and what was the endpoint?
Now I want to focus on the transfer outcome. Take me back to the riskiest week — what was the actual situation, and what did you do?
And what was the result of that intervention — for the customer and for the line?

candidate_last_answer: So the riskiest week was when we discovered, during the first qualification run on the transferred fluid-management line, that the moves had shifted a critical seal-weld parameter window — the equipment behaved differently than at the sending site even though the spec was identical. The situation was that we had a customer build commitment about three weeks out and the line couldn't hold the weld within validated limits. What I did was pull the process engineer, the equipment tech, and the sending-site SME into a focused room, and we ran a structured comparison of the two setups parameter by parameter until we found the fixture-clamping pressure differed. I redirected our maintenance priority entirely onto re-characterizing that station, and I personally owned the daily standup on it and kept Quality in the loop on whether we'd need a re-validation.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: kept Quality in the loop on whether we'd need a re-validation

expected-yield: the actual result — whether re-validation was required, whether the three-week customer build was hit on time, and what would have happened to the commitment had the clamping-pressure root cause not been found that week
